metadata.seed: I feel like. I don't know. I don't really even deserve to be hear if I can't do well enough. I see all these other students who are doing well and doing better and want to be there. I don't know why I'm here, even at uni, to be honest.

patient.query: I feel like. I don't know. I don't really even deserve to be hear if I can't do well enough. I see all these other students who are doing well and doing better and want to be there. I don't know why I'm here, even at uni, to be honest.

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy sense that you’re not good enough, and that’s making you question your place here. What evidence do you have that you’re a failure, and can you think of any moments where you’ve actually succeeded or made progress? If a close friend were in your shoes, what might they say to help you see a different perspective?

patient.query: Yeah, it does feel heavy sometimes, like there’s this weight I can’t shake off. I think about my job, and how I just keep messing things up or feeling like I’m just coasting by. But then, I guess there have been moments when I’ve done well, like when I completed that project last month. I don’t know, it almost feels like those moments don’t count in the grand scheme of things. If it were a friend, they might say I’m too

neutral: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy sense of doubt, even when you’ve achieved something meaningful. That mix of feeling like you’re “messing up” and then questioning the value of your successes can feel exhausting. It’s understandable that this weight would linger and make it hard to see the good moments as truly significant.

patient.query: Yeah, it’s like I can’t just enjoy those good moments without that nagging voice in my head. It’s almost as if I’m waiting for the other shoe to drop. I keep worrying that I’ll disappoint myself again, or that people will see right through me. I sometimes wonder if I’m just pretending to be capable when I know deep down I’m not.

entailment: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of worry about how others see you and how you see yourself. What evidence do you have that you’re disappointing yourself or that people will see right through you? Are there moments when you’ve felt capable or when others have seen you differently? How might you view those moments differently?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing
retrieved_concepts_used.label: entailment

patient.query: I guess I’ve had my moments, like when my boss complimented my work or when friends reach out to me for help. But those feelings of doubt always creep back in, and it’s hard to focus on the good. I just think, “What if they were just being nice?” or “What if I won’t be able to keep it up?” It’s like I’m always waiting for someone to call me out, and I don’t know how to shake that off.

entailment: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re worried that the praise might not be genuine or that you might not sustain it. What evidence do you have that the compliments were just niceness? What evidence suggests you could keep up the level of work? How might you view the situation if you considered other possibilities?

patient.query: I don’t know… I guess I just feel like people say nice things because they have to, or maybe they’re just being polite. I mean, I’ve always been the “nice one” who helps out, but that doesn’t mean I’m really good at what I do, right? And it’s hard to imagine being consistent when I feel like I’m barely holding it together most days. Sometimes I think if I just let my guard down, it’ll all come crashing down. But

entailment: Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)
neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Tends to be critical of others (finding)
contradiction: Tends to appear indifferent to praise or criticism (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re questioning whether the kindness you show is genuine or just a way to keep things smooth. What evidence do you have that people say nice things only out of obligation, and how does that shape how you see yourself? If you let your guard down, what specific worries come up about how you’ll be perceived or how you’ll handle the day?

patient.query: It’s just… I don’t know, it feels like I’ve always been the one trying to keep the peace. I guess I’ve had moments where I felt close to people, and they’d say something nice, but then I’d think, “Maybe they just don’t want to hurt my feelings.” It makes me doubt my worth, like I’m just this facade. If I let my guard down, I worry that people will see that I’m not as strong or capable as they think

entailment: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)
neutral: Fear of speaking to people in authority (finding)
Fear of being in a small group (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re worried that showing your true self might make others see you as less capable. What evidence do you have that people actually view you that way, and can you think of moments when they responded differently? Exploring those examples might help you see a more balanced picture of how you’re perceived.

patient.query: I guess I just… I’ve always been hard on myself. The times I remember getting praise, there’s this little voice saying it was just luck or a momentary thing. Like at that team meeting when I shared my ideas and people seemed to like them, but then I thought, “What if they were just being nice because they felt sorry for me?” It’s like I keep looking for proof that I’m not good enough, and it’s hard to focus on the good stuff when the

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re doubting the sincerity of the praise you’ve received. What evidence do you have that the positive feedback was just a one‑off or a courtesy? Can you think of other occasions where your contributions were genuinely valued, or where people followed up on your ideas?

patient.query: I mean, I guess there have been times when people have come back to me for input on projects or mentioned how my ideas helped. But then, I start to wonder if they just did that out of obligation or if they were just trying to keep me involved. It feels easier to think that way, because if I believe I actually did something good, then I have to live up to it again. And that’s just… overwhelming.

neutral: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy sense of pressure to prove your worth, which can feel exhausting. What evidence do you have that people are only involved out of obligation, rather than genuine appreciation of your ideas? You might consider whether your contributions are valued for their own sake, not just to meet an expectation you feel you must uphold.

patient.query: Sometimes, I think about those moments when people liked my ideas, and I just feel this pressure. I start to wonder if they were just being polite or if they really meant it. It feels safer to think that way, because if I believe I’ve done something good, then I have to keep living up to that, and that just feels so overwhelming. I guess I just keep worrying that I’m one mistake away from being seen as a fraud.

neutral: Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)
Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of pressure to prove yourself every time someone shows interest in your ideas. What evidence do you have that the positive reactions were just polite, rather than genuine appreciation? If you could view the situation from a more balanced perspective, how might that change the way you feel about meeting that high standard?

patient.query: Yeah, I guess I don’t really have proof that it was all just politeness. I mean, maybe they actually liked what I said. But it’s just hard to believe it when I feel so uncertain about myself. Thinking about it from a different angle… it’s scary to imagine that they could really appreciate me for who I am. It makes me feel like I’d have to try harder to keep that up, and I don’t know if I can handle the pressure. That just makes

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Fear of speaking to people in authority (finding)

llm_response.response:
It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of pressure to keep up that image, and that can feel overwhelming.  
Do you think it’s truly necessary to always perform at that high level for others to appreciate you?  
Perhaps being authentic and showing who you really are could be just as, if not more, valued than maintaining a perfect façade.
